strong: J: Daniel 7 'son of man' is the formative reception trajectory through 1 Enoch 46-48 (Parables) -> 4 Ezra 13 -> Mark 13/Matt 24 -> Rev 1/13/14 -- Beale's signature thesis demonstrated by detailed allusion-density and structural-pattern analysis.
N: Revelation 13 has been moulded by the threefold authorization scheme of Daniel 7:3-6, 13-14; Daniel is 'the most formative influence on the thought and structure of Revelation.'

C: Rev 13 beast = ironic re-working / parody of the Daniel 7 fourth-beast complex and the 'son of man' authorization, inheriting little-horn warring-against-saints and forty-two-month / time-times-half-time periods.
O: Inaugurated-eschatological / 'already and not yet' fulfillment structure; Johannine claims described as 'beginning fulfillments of Daniel's prophecy' rather than purely future.
moderate: B: Implicit Roman identification of the fourth-kingdom horns/heads (notes the 'usual' identification of seven mountains with the seven hills of Rome without strongly arguing it).
K (method): Apocalyptists, including John, respect O.T. context; against the older 'atomistic' caricature of apocalyptic exegesis.
L: Mark 13 / Synoptic Olivet discourse is itself a Danielic midrash that mediates Daniel into Revelation (following Hartman, Farrer, Sweet, Vos).
tentative: Synchronous-parallel reading of Revelation's vision-cycles (seals/trumpets/bowls) corresponding to the parallel structure of Daniel's five visions (Dan 2; 7; 8; 9; 10-12).

positions.position: Dan-7 'son of man' is the formative reception-history pivot: through 1 Enoch's Parables (chs. 46-48 as midrash on Dan 7:9-27), 4 Ezra 13 (Dan 2 stone + Dan 7 man combined), Mark 13/Matt 24 Olivet-discourse Danielic-midrash, into Rev 1:7, 1:13-20, 13, 14:14, 17. Christ as 'son of man' is identified with the suffering/reigning saints (Dan 7:18, 22, 27) yet retains individuality.
positions.rationale: Beale traces the trajectory inductively. In 1 Enoch 46-47 he identifies ~13 Daniel allusions in two chapters and a parallel 7-element thought-structure with Daniel 7, concluding the chapters constitute a 'midrash' on Dan 7:9-27 that creatively develops the 'son of man's' role as judge -- a development from the 'judgment given to the saints' of Dan 7:22 not yet explicit in Daniel itself. In Rev 1, vv. 8-20 are likewise a 'midrash' on Daniel 7 and 10, with Dan 7 'more influential than Daniel 10', and Rev 1:5-7, 1:9, 1:13-16, 5:7-13, 17:14 identify Christ-as-Son-of-Man with the saints in suffering and reign. Mediated through a Christian-Danielic tradition reflected in the Synoptic Olivet discourse.

positions.position: Daniel is the most formative O.T. influence on the thought and structure of the Apocalypse -- exceeding Ezekiel, Isaiah and Zechariah. This is shown by (a) Danielic models / Vorbilder structuring Rev 1; 4-5; 13; 17; (b) Dan 2:28-29, 45 allusions in Rev 1:1, 1:19, 4:1, 22:6 as macro-structural sign-posts; (c) shared three-part Leitmotiv (cosmic eschatological judgment, divine sovereignty, faithful saints under tribulation); (d) high allusion density (50% of Rev 1:7-20, ~two-thirds of Rev 13).
positions.rationale: Beale's overall thesis. He builds it cumulatively across four chapter-length exegeses (Rev 1; 4-5; 13; 17), demonstrating in each that a Danielic 'Vorbild' organizes the imagery and that other O.T. references are drawn into the Daniel framework via thematic/catchword links acting as a 'hermeneutical magnet.' The Dan 2:28-29 allusion-cluster (1:1, 1:19, 4:1, 22:6) is read as deliberate macro-marker dividing the book into intro / churches / vision-proper / conclusion.

positions.position: Rev 13 beast is a creative re-working of the Dan 7 four-beast complex with the 'little horn' character collapsed into the singular beast: the 'seven heads' are a composite of the four Daniel beasts; ten horns/diadems are Daniel's ten kings (Dan 7:24); blasphemous mouth, war on saints, forty-two-month time-period are Dan 7:25 + Dan 12:7. The whole functions as an ironic parody of the Dan 7:14 Son-of-Man authorization.
positions.rationale: The Rev 13 exegesis (CH3.3.1) annotates verse-by-verse Dan-7 allusion density and concludes Rev 13 is 'modelled on Daniel 7' via Daniel 7:3-6, 13-14's 'threefold authorization scheme' (agent steps forward -> power given -> effect). Beale stresses ironic re-application: the same wording Daniel uses for the Son of Man's reception of universal worship (7:14) is applied to the beast, making the beast a 'parody of the son of man's final triumph.'

positions.position: Inaugurated / 'already and not yet': Christ's reign and the saints' kingdom are described as 'beginning fulfillments' of Daniel's prophecy -- Christ's death-resurrection inaugurate the Dan-7 son-of-man-and-saints reign, with consummation still future. The five Daniel visions (Dan 2; 7; 8; 9; 10-12) are read synchronously, and Beale reads Revelation's vision-cycles (seals/trumpets/bowls) as similarly recapitulatory rather than strictly chronological.
positions.rationale: Beale commits explicitly to inaugurated eschatology: the Dan-7 prophecy of the son-of-man and saints 'has its initial fulfillment in the overcoming of both through suffering and apparent defeat' beginning at the cross/resurrection; 'for the saints, it occurs throughout the church age until the victory is finally completed.' Revelation's vision cycles parallel rather than succeed each other, on analogy with Daniel's parallel visions.

positions.position: John's use of Daniel in Revelation is mediated, at least in part, by an early Christian Danielic-midrashic tradition reflected in the Olivet discourse (Mark 13 / Matt 24 / Luke 21). Following Hartman, Farrer, Sweet and Vos, Beale argues that Mark 13 is itself a coherent meditation on Dan 7-9, 11-12, and that John's repeated Danielic models build on this Christian-Daniel tradition rather than purely on Daniel directly.
positions.rationale: Beale endorses Hartman's case that 'the main part of the eschatological discourse [in Mark 13 and Matthew 24] is based on a coherent exposition of or meditation on' texts from Daniel 7-9, 11-12, and notes that Synoptic allusions occur in Revelation precisely where Beale has independently identified Dan-based models (Rev 1, Rev 6:2-17 woes, Rev 13:7-8). Hedges on directness -- 'whether John knew the Synoptics or a pre-synoptic tradition' -- but commits to the mediation.

positions.position: The 'saints' of Dan 7:18, 22, 27 are read corporately and Christologically through Revelation: their reign is identified with the Son of Man's reign (per Dan 7), and Rev 1:5-9; 5:7-13; 17:14 explicitly fuse the two. The Christian community as 'maskilim' from Dan 11:33-35 / 12:3, 10 is identified with the saints in Rev 13:18 and 17:9 who exercise discerning 'wisdom' against the beast.
positions.rationale: Beale reads the saints corporately, with Christ as their representative head ('John identifies the victory and reign of Christ -- the son of man -- with that of the saints'). The wisdom/insight references in Rev 13:18 and 17:9 are read as the church standing in the role of the eschatological wise (maskilim) of Daniel's persecuted faithful, with Rev 13:9-10 functioning as a Danielic exhortation to perseverance under persecution.

quote.text: John associates those who go along with pagan society with

positions.position: Apocalyptic = revelation of secrets concerning future events of history or hidden divine operation, typically in vision form. Apocalyptic authors, including Qumran, 1 Enoch, 4 Ezra, 2 Baruch and John, exhibit a strong tendency to respect the broader O.T. context from which their allusions are drawn -- i.e., 'contextual' rather than 'atomistic' use, against the older Strack-Billerbeck-style caricature.
positions.rationale: Beale's methodological contribution: by inductively examining the surrounding allusions (not just the cited verse) in each apocalyptic context studied, he demonstrates a recurring 'Danielic Vorbild' or controlling pattern within which other O.T. references cohere thematically. The corollary is that to read apocalyptic well, one must inquire whether a given allusion is part of a larger source-context pattern.

positions.position: Implicit Roman identification: Beale notes the standard identification of the seven mountains of Rev 17:9 with the seven hills of Rome without strongly arguing for a specific kingdom-number identification of Daniel 7's fourth beast. His main move is collapsing Dan 7's four-beast complex into the single Rev 13/17 beast (universal eschatological world-system) rather than committing to a Greek-vs-Roman fourth-kingdom thesis.
positions.rationale: Beale focuses on reception trajectory rather than the Maccabean-vs-Roman dating debate over Daniel itself. In Rev 17 he treats the seven heads as 'usually identified by commentators with the seven hills of Rome' alongside a more cosmic reading, and concludes the symbol is 'closely identified with the world political system' without forcing a single referent.

quote.text: The crucial issue for the author of Revelation is that of true worship of the legitimate divine sovereign

crossBookReadings.positionSummary: Rev 1:7-20 is a Dan-7 + Dan-10 midrash. Roughly 50% of O.T. references in vv. 7-20 come from Daniel; v. 7 cites Dan 7:13 + Zech 12:10 (perhaps via Matt 24:30); vv. 12-16 fuse the Daniel 7 (Ancient of Days, son-of-man) and Daniel 10 (white-robed heavenly figure) descriptions and apply both to the risen Christ. A nine-element image-sequence in Rev 1:4-20 parallels Daniel 7 (throne, surrounding heavenly beings, son-of-man's universal rule, saints made a kingdom, coming on clouds, book of judgment, detailed description, seer's distress, interpretation). Daniel 2:28-29 in Rev 1:1, 1:19 frames the whole as Danielic mystery-revelation.

crossBookReadings.positionSummary: Rev 13 is moulded on Daniel 7 throughout. Two-thirds of O.T. references come from Daniel; vv. 1-2 are 'a creative re-working of Daniel 7:1-7' (the seven heads composite the four Dan-7 beasts; ten diadems = Dan 7:24's ten kings). The whole chapter follows Dan 7's threefold authorization-scheme (agent steps forward -> power conferred -> effect on world), repeated for both first beast (vv. 1-8) and second beast (vv. 11-17). Most strikingly, Dan 7:14 (Son-of-Man's reception of universal authority and worship) is ironically applied to the beast in Rev 13:7b-8a, making the beast an anti-Christ parody -- confirmed by 'wounded head' (13:3) and 'horns like a lamb' (13:11) as parodies of the slain Lamb in Rev 5.

crossBookReadings.positionSummary: Rev 17 reprises and develops the Dan-7 beast complex with further ironic Daniel use. Rev 17:8's 'was, is not, is about to come up out of the abyss and goes to destruction' is a collective paraphrase of Dan 7:3, 11, 17, 23, 26 -- the threefold rise/destruction of the Dan-7 beast(s) -- set as ironic parody of the divine 'who was and is and is to come.' Rev 17:14a uses Dan 7:21 (horn warring against saints) with 'polemical irony': now the Lamb conquers the beast with the same warring-language. The 'King of kings, Lord of lords' title (17:14b) derives from Dan 4:37 (LXX). The ten horns = Dan 7:24's ten kings; the seven heads = Dan 7:4-7 composite.

crossBookReadings.positionSummary: 1 Enoch 46-48 (within the Parables / Similitudes, dated no later than late first century AD) is a midrash on Daniel 7:9-27. Beale tabulates ~13 Daniel allusions across 1 En 46-47 and identifies a parallel 7-element thought-structure (Head of Days, son-of-man's designation for judgment, judgment on persecutors, persecutors' sins, saints' deliverance/prayer, vindication). The most outstanding non-Danielic development is the Son of Man's role as active judge -- an extension Beale traces to Dan 7:22's 'judgment given for the saints.' This grounds the post-Daniel Son-of-Man reception trajectory.

crossBookReadings.positionSummary: Following Hartman, Farrer and Sweet, Beale endorses the view that Mark 13 (with parallels in Matt 24 / Luke 21) is itself a Danielic midrash 'based on a coherent exposition of or meditation on' Daniel 7-9 and 11-12. This tradition -- what Beale calls 'a Christian-Daniel tradition' -- mediates Daniel into Revelation, explaining the convergence of Synoptic-discourse parallels with Beale's independently-identified Daniel-models in Rev 1, Rev 6 (woes), and Rev 13. Rev 1:1 / 1:19 / 4:1's 'what must take place' phrase echoes the same Dan 2:28-29, 45 phrase used in Mark 13:7 / Matt 24:6 / Luke 21:9.

crossBookReadings.positionSummary: Matt 24:30's combination of Dan 7:13 (the coming Son-of-Man on clouds) with Zech 12:10 (mourning of all tribes) likely 'suggested' to John the same combination at Rev 1:7. The Olivet discourse stands in the same Christian-Danielic tradition as Mark 13 (see Mark 13 entry); Matt 24:24's false-prophet language is paralleled in Rev 13:11ff..

frontend.author: Beale
frontend.title: The Use of Daniel in Jewish Apocalyptic Literature and in the Revelation of St. John
frontend.section: §CH3.1.1.1 — a. Introduction to the Vision (1:7ff.)
frontend.citationString: Beale, Use of Daniel in Revelation, §CH3.1.1.1 (pp. 410–412)

quote.text: Revelation 1:7a speaks of the eschatological coming of Christ by referring to Daniel 7:13 and Zechariah 12:10–12; Matthew 24:30 may have suggested this combination to John

distinctiveMoves: Methodological insistence that O.T. allusions in apocalyptic literature carry their broader contexts with them ('contextual' rather than 'atomistic' usage), demonstrated inductively across Qumran, 1 Enoch, 4 Ezra, 2 Baruch and Revelation.
Treats Daniel 2 allusions in Rev 1:1, 1:19, 4:1, 22:6 as deliberate 'sign-posts' marking the macro-structure of the Apocalypse.
Identifies an ironic / parodic re-application of Dan 7:14 (Son-of-Man authorization) to the beast in Rev 13:7-8 -- the beast as anti-Son-of-Man.
Argues the 'son of man as judge' development in 1 Enoch's Parables is a creative (non-Danielic) extension of Dan 7:22's 'judgment given for the saints' construct.
Treats Rev 1:12-20 not just as a Dan-10 christophany but as a Dan-7+10 midrash, with a nine-element shared image-sequence demonstrating Dan-7 dominance.

Reads the beast complex (Rev 13/17) corporately and chronologically: 'seven heads' = composite of all four Dan-7 beasts, with its own Christ-parody arc (was/is-not/coming up vs. Christ's death/resurrection/parousia).
uncertainties: Does not adjudicate the 'on-going son of man debate' in Gospels research (explicitly brackets it in CH1.2).
Holds the 'whether John knew the Synoptics directly or via a pre-synoptic Danielic tradition' question open -- only that some Christian-Daniel tradition mediates.
Hedges on whether 1 Enoch 48 forms part of the Dan-7 midrash with chs. 46-47 ('it is just as possible that any dominance of Daniel 7 has ceased').
Says little about the dating of Daniel itself (work treats Daniel as the source-text without entering Maccabean-vs-sixth-century date debates).
